issue_title: [ENH] conda info to report LD_LIBRARY_PATH or DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH
issue_body: We need to try and help people help themselves and to head off common causes of Anaconda/Conda failure, such as LD_LIBRARY_PATH and DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  Longer term a `conda debug` command might be useful (similarly a GUI version in Navigator), but in the short term it would probably go a long way to helping us if we reported `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` or `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` with the output of `conda info`.

issue_title: Using Anaconda Cloud message should say Anaconda Repo
issue_body: When doing a conda search against a local Anaconda Repo, conda shows the message Using Anaconda Cloud api: <repo channel>.  Can we change the message to say Anaconda Repo instead of Anaconda Cloud.  It would be more accurate and it would not raise red flags within the enterprise about accessing external (Internet/Cloud) resources.

issue_title: new auxlib needs to call git subprocess with cwd set
issue_body: @kalefranz the new auxlib just gave me the strangest error, which took me a while to figure out.  The reason was because I was working on a project (constructor) which is also under git revision control.  After some debugging, I realized that calling `git` in the new `auxlib` is the problem.  You need to ensure that all subprocess calls to `git` use the `cwd` keyword argument (with value set to the conda root directory).  Otherwise `git` is called on whatever other project you happen to work on, and you get errors which are hard to debug.

issue_title: default to channel-priority = False
issue_body:
The current (new) default changes the old behaviour of conda which means that a lot of users will be surprised when they update to the new conda versions (I certainly was :-/).

IMO it's also a more sane behaviour: currently the priorities mean that newer versions gets downgraded on updates which is not what I want when I use both conda-forge and defaults. 

Its also potentially harmfull as new new channels are added as the highest priority and so this channel can bring back old packages: with the old conda versions and a user adding a channel for "just one newer package", old leftover packages are not used -> New channels were only harmful when the channel owner explicitly would add a harmful package with a newer version number but now a not updated test package can happen to be installed.

It would also be nice if the help would tell one how to influence channel priority.

The current option is also advertised as beta and subject to change which is IMO an argument to not make it active on default.

```
 --channel-priority, --channel-pri, --chan-pri
                        Channel priority takes precedence over packaage
                        version (default: True). Note: This feature is in beta
                        and may change in a future release.
```

[`packaage` -> `package`?]

issue_title: Setting alleles removes END by overwriting rlen
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm not sure whether this is intended behavior, but setting the `ref` or `alts` attributes of a `VariantRecord` removes the record's `stop`/`END`. The alleles setter method and the ref setter method overwrite the record's `rlen` with the length of the reference allele. In the case of symbolic alleles, this removes the true `rlen`. issue_title: [DNS] Long TXT strings need to be broken up
issue_body:
When creating a TXT record, if a very long string (over 255 characters) is provided, it needs to be broken up into separate strings of up to 255 characters each. A single TXT record is still created (a single TXT record contains an array of strings).

For example, this should work:
`az network dns record txt add -g rg1 -z jt-test.com --record-set-name cli2txt --value 123456789a123456789b123456789c123456789d123456789e123456789f123456789g123456789h123456789i123456789j123456789k123456789l123456789m123456789n123456789o123456789p123456789q123456789r123456789s123456789t123456789u123456789v123456789w123456789x123456789y123456789z
`

issue_title: [Network] BGP Peering Address should not be settable on any gateway commands
issue_body:
A comment carried over from PR #2751. `az network vnet/local-gateway create/update` commands expose the `bgp_peering_address` property, which is set automatically by the service and not intended to be configurable. 

In this case, this property should be set as ReadOnly in the swagger spec (https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/issues/1115)

issue_title: Remove Az acs create requires --location parameter
issue_body: @yugangw-msft if user has to create a resource group first, then the location becomes meaningless in the az acs create command. In fact, if a user provides a different location than the one provided during the resource group creation, the create command will fail.

issue_title: Delete a repository from Azure Container Registry
issue_body:
It is not currently possible to delete an image from a repository in Azure Container Registry, either through the UI or the CLI tool -- other than manually looking at all of the digests for all of the layers of a specific image, and deleting those through the blob filesystem (this is an unreasonable method of deletion, prone to human error).

The registry API supports deleting images (all image layers): https://docs.docker.com/registry/spec/api/#deleting-an-image

`az acr repository` should support deleting an image repository from a specific registry, perhaps in the format of:

```bash
az acr repository delete --name MyRegistry --repo someRepo/someImage
```

issue_title: [Storage] Support downloading multiple blobs/files with a single call

Since blob names allow slashes to simulate directories for organizational purposes, it would be nice if we could download multiple blobs by specifying a prefix (ex: CreateVM_2016-07-01*). This would require the downloaded file name to default as described in issue #995.

For files, you would download multiple files not by specifying a file prefix (although you could), but by specifying the directory path.

issue_title: Handler running ProcessPoolExecutor fail to close socket
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is a standard example handling heavy lift tasks with ProcessPoolExecutor.  The executor successfully finishes the task but fail to close the TCP connection.

The same problem does not happen with ThreadPoolExecutor.

## Expected behavior

when keep-alive is off. Server should close the connection when the response is sent. 

## Actual behaviour

The tcp connection remains in 'established state' as reported by netstat. Tracing suggests the transport.close has been called.
`    
 > /Users/tingfan/umbo/VisionDolphin/python/cvworker/.env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/web_protocol.py(515)start()
    514                 if self.transport is not None:
--> 515                     self.transport.close()
    516 
`
web_protocol.py:115

## Steps to reproduce

`#!/usr/bin/env python
from aiohttp import web
import time
import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor

executor = ProcessPoolExecutor(1)
loop=asyncio.get_event_loop()

async def handler(request):
    await loop.run_in_executor(executor, time.sleep, 0.1)
    print('done')
    return web.Response(text='resp')

app=web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', handler)
web.run_app(app, host='localhost', port=8080)
`

In another shell use apache bench to connect. It will hang instead of complete the test.
`
ab -n 1 http://127.0.0.1:8080/
`

What's interesting is if one make any new request to the same server. The new request will be processed without issue. Only the first request on a fresh server will hang.

## Your environment
OSX 10.12.5 (16F73)
Ubuntu 16.04
python 3.5.2 / 3.6.1
aiohttp==2.3.0a0 
`

issue_title: 'DeprecationWarning: no_delay option is deprecated' raised even if 'no_delay' argument was not provided
issue_body:
After update to aiomysql 0.0.5 I've receive such warning:
'/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiomysql/connection.py:154: DeprecationWarning: no_delay option is deprecated'
It was very strange because I've not provide such argument anywhere.

After diving deep a little bit I've discovered that such warning was added in this commit - [link to commit](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiomysql/commit/364aa6d7618b4a83ff96c77806ee797d181a4602#diff-b4987acea49665e9f985c0da9f5604f4)

There on line 147 was added restriction
```
        if no_delay is not None:
            warnings.warn("no_delay option is deprecated", DeprecationWarning)
            no_delay = bool(no_delay)
        else:
            no_delay = True
```
But at the same time default argument at __init__ is `no_delay=False`.
So with that settings it raise DeprecationWarning any time you create some connection.
I'd like to fix it, but I cannot figure out what right default 'no_delay' value should be.

Versions:
aiomysql: 0.0.5
python: 3.5.0
MariaDB: mysql  Ver 15.1 Distrib 10.1.8-MariaDB
Ubuntu 14.04

issue_title: [RFC] Deprecated <tree> attributes
issue_body:
`string` attribute in `<tree>` node is [deprecated since 8.0](https://github.com/odoo/odoo/blob/420e433fa3a6b671868fa578e6006dd215570706/doc/reference/views.rst#lists) (no longer displayed) and `colors` and `fonts` are [deprecated since 9.0](https://github.com/odoo/odoo/blob/420e433fa3a6b671868fa578e6006dd215570706/doc/reference/views.rst#lists) in favor of `decoration-{$name}`.

I went ahead an implemented a crude version of it, however, there is the question if something like this would likely be integrated into _pylint-odoo_?

If yes, then I have a few further questions:

1. Since the rule deals with certain versions of Odoo only, the check should be skipped in other versions in order to avoid false positives. I saw [`valid_odoo_versions` config value being used](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/blob/d0b0d100057666f21bbb7b84c1779e2c02ca8c80/pylint_odoo/checkers/modules_odoo.py#L770-L771) in one place. Is this a good/right approach?
2. What is the policy for comming up with new message codes? Do I simply increment the highest existing code by one?

issue_title: TypeError: bad operand type for abs(): 'FloatNode'
issue_body:
Hi, your work is totally amazing :) I have been using it for a few weeks already and I faced this "bad operand type" issue recently. I have double-checked everything and I'm not using numpy anywhere. I'm little confused with what exactly might have went wrong and I'd be happy to present any working (well, not working, actually :) ) example, but right now it's just unclear what exactly to copy-paste;

Here's the actual loss, `bk == autograd.numpy` here, `data` and `theta` are `namedtuple` (the packing-unpacking part works just fine with `autograd` here, tested with other losses):

    def predict(self, theta, data):
        const = assert_arr_shape({
            data.X.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_steps, self.n_input_dim),
            data.y.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_hidden_dim)
        })
        h_prev = bk.repeat(theta.h_0[:, bk.newaxis], const.batch_size, axis=1).T
        activation = lambda x: bk.maximum(0, 1-x)
        to_stack = []
        for i in range(self.n_steps):
            input2hidden = bk.dot(data.X[:, i, :], theta.W_input_to_hidden)
            hidden2hidden = bk.dot(h_prev, theta.W_hidden_to_hidden)
            before_activation = (input2hidden + hidden2hidden)
            h_prev = activation(before_activation)
            to_stack.append(h_prev)
        output = bk.stack(to_stack, axis=1)
        assert_arr_shape({output.shape: (None, self.n_steps, self.n_hidden_dim)})
        
        return output

    def loss(self, theta, data):
        pred = self.predict(theta, data)
        last_step_pred = pred[:, -1, :]
        loss = bk.sum(bk.abs(last_step_pred - data.y))
        return loss

The forward pass (`loss(x)`, `x`-ravel'ed everything) executes just fine, but when I try computing the gradient (`autograd.grad(loss)(x)`), this exception raises.

It does indeed tries to apply `abs()` on the np.ndarray that has `FloatNode` objects instead of actual floats in it.

Thank you!

Ben

issue_title: Operations taking CryptographicParameters fail if set to default None
issue_body:
If you call `encrypt` (or I think anything else taking a `cryptographic_parameters` argument) without specifying the `cryptographic_parameters`, an exception is thrown:

```client.encrypt(b"some data", uuid)```

raises

```
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/pie/client.py", line 1252, in _build_cryptographic_parameters
    raise TypeError("Cryptographic parameters must be a dictionary.")
```

The problem is `encrypt` checks for and accepts the default None, but it then passes its argument to `self._build_cryptographic_parameters` which accepts only a dict.

I think all that is required is to add something like

if value is None:
   value = {}

at the start of `_build_cryptographic_parameters`

issue_title: Explicitly log uncaught exceptions with juju-log at ERROR level
issue_body: It seems that the default logging level for models was reduced some time back.  This makes the logs much less noisy and saves quite a bit of space, but it has the unfortunate side effect of making debugging charm failures from logs more difficult, as most failure messages only show up as stderr output.  It would probably be worth adding explicit logging of uncaught exceptions to the reactive framework so that they are clearly visible in the logs.

issue_title: Add convenience method for accessing config
issue_body:
Something like

```python
class Header:
    ...
    def config_data(self, obj_name):
        """
        Example
        --------
        Get the device configuration recorded for the object det.
        >>> h.config_data('det')
        {'primary': [{'exposure_time': 1.0}]}
        """
        result = defaultdict(list)
        for d in self.descriptors:
            config = d['configuration'].get(obj_name)
            if config:
                result[d['name']].append(config['data'])
        return dict(result)  # strip off defaultdict behavior
```

issue_title: Make fill=False by default (for all functions)
issue_body:
It sounds like @tacaswell, @klauer, and I all agree on this. OK with everyone else?

Proximate Motivation: `restream` and `process` pass the documents to bluesky callbacks, which expect the *un-filled* documents that the RunEngine provides. Filled Documents cause them to blow up when the try to (redundant) fill them.

General Motivation: Filling is expensive and we shouldn't do it by default.

Is there any chance I can bait someone else into looking at this? @arkilic or @ericdill? I could use a break from databroker work for awhile, but this should get done soon.

issue_title: Unwanted I202 errors
issue_body:
Hey there, we're using this plugin for linting imports at EDITED and have noticed unwanted `I202`s with recent versions.

Our style would normally allow for both of the following cases:
```py
import X
import Y
import Z
from X import *
from X import A
```
 and 
```py
import X
import Y
import Z

from X import *
from X import A
```

with the difference here being the empty line between the `import` and `from` parts of an import section.

It looks like the second case is no longer allowed under `I202` though.
We have several codebases that already follow that kind of style though, and are getting a fair amount of `I202`s that we're actually OK with.

For the time being, we can pin to a version of the plugin that does not flag those up, so it's not a pressing issue.
Do you however have any suggestions on how to proceed here to avoid remaining pinned to a version permanently?
If there are any pointers on necessary changes to allow for a bit more flexibility around this, I could provide a PR.

issue_title: Why is ASM Violations collection not available?
issue_body:
Hi,

I am wondering how can I get a collection of all ASM violations?

To me, the fact that I am able to fetch them via this REST call:
   `https://<hostname>/mgmt/tm/asm/violations/`
should be a proof that the F5 API does support this collection.

Why is it then not implemented in f5-common-python?
And why is it also missing in ASM namespace in official [user guide](https://devcentral.f5.com/d/the-user-guide-for-the-icontrol-rest-interface-in-big-ip-version-1160?download=true) (page 71)?

I don't understand why is it missing in the ASM namespace, and also not implemented in f5-common-sdk when the API is able to deliver the data for violations?

What am I missing here?

Thanks in advance for any ideas.

Cheers, Martin

issue_title: The iapp module should allow a user to upload a flat file containing the full iapp template
issue_body: Many F5 customers do not write their own iapp templates. They download them from F5 and tweak settings, then upload the template through the GUI. The iapp.py module should accept a large string block (as though it's been read from a file) that can be parsed into the correct JSON blob to create a template.

issue_title: Net Native compilation fails with Out of Memory (Xamarin/UWP)
issue_body:
We have a big problem with the net native compilation in a relative big project.
The release build only works if we disable the option “Compile with .NET Native tool chain” or “Optimize code”.
Otherwise the build takes up to 10 minutes and ends with an out of memory error.

The build log file is attached: [build.log](https://github.com/dotnet/core/files/1837784/build.log)

There is also an ilcRepro.zip file attached:
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1bhEwZco0fugxxRDxj0hi86jnb9eJIpOV/view?usp=sharing

We tried several different settings in the Default.rd.xml, but nothing worked yet.

•	We comment out the application assembly tag
<!-- <Assembly Name="*Application*" Dynamic="Required All" DoNotOptimize="true"/> -->
•	We also tried to add an extra assembly tag for every assembly and with DoNotOptimize

But neither worked. 

Please give me an advice or another solution. I don’t know if this is a bug or an assembly doesn’t worked or is not ready for net native.

issue_title: ./letsencrypt-auto fails on update because of missing package python2.7-dev
issue_body:
Problem:
```bash
$>cat /etc/issue
Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS \n \l
$> git show
commit 6ea51360f9fcbd40e1c754bbc63a57224b73fb92
$> ./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a webroot --webroot-path /apache/htdocs/ -d example.com --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview
Updating lUpdating letsencrypt and virtual environment dependencies......Command "/home/dune73/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/python2 -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-JtvkAg/cffi/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-yrfsWF-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /home/dune73/.local/share/letsencrypt/include/site/python2.7/cffi" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-JtvkAg/cffietsencrypt and virtual environment dependencies......Command "/home/dune73/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/python2 -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-JtvkAg/cffi/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-yrfsWF-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /home/dune73/.local/share/letsencrypt/include/site/python2.7/cffi" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-JtvkAg/cffi
```
The root-cause:
```bash
$> sudo pip install --upgrade cffi
...
    c/_cffi_backend.c:2:20: fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory
     #include <Python.h>
                        ^
    compilation terminated.
    error: command 'x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc' failed with exit status 1
```

The Solution:

```bash
$> sudo apt-get install pythond2.7-dev
```

issue_title: Django: unable to set campaign when using EmailMessage object
issue_body:
I'm trying to send an email with a campaign id using a `EmailMessage` object as follows:

```
msg = EmailMessage(
    subject='Test Campaign',
    to=['test@test.com']
)
msg.template = 'test-template'
msg.substitution_data = substitution_data
msg.campaign = 'test-campaign'
msg.send()
```

Viewing the message in the SparkPost Dashboard, I can see the template and data are set, but the campaign remains empty. Following https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost/issues/107, it looks like the problem is in the `SparkPostMessage` class. `substitution_data`, `template` are copied there, but not `campaign`.

issue_title: Add get-file-info-by-name command.
issue_body:
The quickest and cheapest way to get information about a file is to use a `HEAD` request on the URL that downloads a file by name.  This is faster that getting the file info using `b2_get_file_info` and the file ID.  (The B2 internals are optimized for accessing files by name, which is the most common use case.)

It would be nice to have a `get-file-info-by-name` option in the command-line tool that returns the same information as `get-file-info` for the most recent version of a file with a given name.  It should use a `HEAD` request for the download-by-name API, which means that hidden files will get a 404 and will look like they're not there.

issue_title: Heartbeat problems when running with caffe
issue_body:
Background: I'm running my experiments using Caffe.
I had this weird error that my experiments would not terminate and heartbeat signals got lost without notice. It seems that this is due to the main thread accessing C++ stuff through boost.python bindings and that the heartbeat thread was simply not responding.

I was able to fix the problem by changing the IntervalTimer class
https://github.com/IDSIA/sacred/blob/d71cf7c6294e8b7d0c2a3992bfdf1eaf4cf00652/sacred/utils.py#L443
to inherit from `multiprocessing.Process` instead of `threading.Thread`.

If there's interest for adding this I can add a PR. This change should not break any existing functionality and allow for running experiments which use the boost.python interface.

This issue I believe is related to #290. I am using Sacred's master branch and get a key error of config entry not being used anywhere when setting a nested ingredient config while using a named config. Here's a minimal code:

```python
# sample.py

from sacred import Experiment, Ingredient


foo = Ingredient('foo')

@foo.config
def foo_cfg():
    a = 10


bar = Ingredient('bar', ingredients=[foo])

@bar.config
def bar_cfg():
    b = 20


ex = Experiment(ingredients=[foo, bar])

@ex.config
def default():
    c = 20

@ex.named_config
def named():
    c = 30

@ex.automain
def main():

```

Running `python sample.py print_config with named foo.a=40 -f` gives

Hi Christoph,

Could you please have a look at the below test-case?

```
(set-info :smt-lib-version 2.6)

(set-info :status unsat)

(declare-const x Float32)
(assert (= x (fp #b1 #b00000010 #b01100001000011000001011)))
;; This is a small normal number
;; Rational(-11568651, 356811923176489970264571492362373784095686656)

(declare-const y Float32)
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00000000 #b10110100101110000011101)))
;; This is a small subnormal number
;; Rational(-5921821, 713623846352979940529142984724747568191373312)

;; r = (f.add RNA x y)
;;
;;          nextDown(r)        nextUp(r)
;;         /         r        /
;;        /         /        /        x        y     -0 +0
;; <------|----|-|--|--------|-- .. --|-- .. --|-- .. |--|------>
;;             / \
;;            /   \
;;   halfpoint     (+ (to_real x) (to_real y))
;;
;; Given the above picture, I believe r should be r, and definitely
;; not nextUp(r).

(declare-const result Float32)
(assert (= result (fp.add RNA x y)))
(assert (not (= result (fp #b1 #b00000010 #b10111011011010000011010))))
(check-sat)
(exit)
```

In particular, Z3 (4d07fa5db3eb52bcadb0ab618699d2de3fb4addb) thinks that the result is actually nextUp(result). I know RNA results are awkward to check, so I am also definitely not ruling out a mistake on my end :)

issue_title: help into making an autoencoder
issue_body:
Hi,

I have tweaked around with Lasagne and I found it an excellent library. My experiments revolve around building an autoencoder for the MNIST dataset. Unfortunately I didn't manage to train it properly, maybe you can spot any mistakes I made?

Here is the structure I chose:

 * input layer (28x28)
 * 2D convolutional layer, filter size 7x7
 * Max Pooling layer, size 3x3, stride 2x2
 * Dense (fully connected) flattening layer, 10 units (this is the bottleneck)
 * Dense (fully connected) layer, 121 units
 * Reshaping layer to 11x11
 * 2D convolutional layer, filter size 3x3
 * 2D Upscaling layer factor 2
 * 2D convolutional layer, filter size 3x3
 * 2D Upscaling layer factor 2
 * 2D convolutional layer, filter size 5x5
 * Feature max pooling (from 31x28x28 to 28x28)

All the 2D convolutional layers have the biases untied, sigmoid activations and 31 filters.

All the fully connected layers have sigmoid activations.

The loss function used is squared error, the updating function is adagrad. The length of the chunk for the learning is 100 samples, multiplied for 1000 epochs.

here is the original post I made on *stats.stackexchange.com*: http://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/176881/cannot-make-this-autoencoder-network-function-properly-with-convolutional-and-m

Thank you for any hint on what might be wrong!

issue_title: Issue with LSTM output
issue_body:
I noticed LSTM layer output is a little bit off. 

Even with sigmoid activation I would sometimes get 1 < output or output < 0. 

The value of the cell to output gate was being added after the activation function when peepholes are enabled.

At line 970~ in recurrent.py
```python
            outgate = self.nonlinearity_outgate(outgate)

            # Compute new cell value
            cell = forgetgate*cell_previous + ingate*cell_input

            if self.peepholes:
                outgate += cell*self.W_cell_to_outgate
```

should be changed to:
```python
            # Compute new cell value
            cell = forgetgate*cell_previous + ingate*cell_input

            if self.peepholes:
                outgate += cell*W_cell_to_outgate
	
            outgate = self.nonlinearity_outgate(outgate)
```

I'm pretty sure there's a way to pull or push or something but I don't know how. No real experience with Github. I hope I helped anyway.

issue_title: Allow (custom) recurrent layers to accept more than one feature dimension
issue_body: As noted by @f0k here https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/294/files#r35102212, currently all the recurrent layers, including the `CustomRecurrentLayer`, squash the input past the third dimension.  The `CustomRecurrentLayer` doesn't need to do that; it should be able to accept N-D inputs and have, e.g., convolutional layers for its input-to-hidden and hidden-to-hidden connections.  I addressed that here: https://github.com/craffel/nntools/commit/1b55bbe9d910b7bc117040286ae0e6bc22053262 but it needs to be updated.  If and when there are more custom recurrent layer types #357, those should also be able to handle more-than-one-feature-dimension input.

issue_body:
Dear nbval-Team,

unfortunately nbval is not working with ``tqdm``. (this is the minimal working example for #55)

While a cell with ``output_type="execute_result"`` seem to always work, like e.g.
```
s = "foo"
s
```
the success of a cell with ``output_type="stream"`` depends on whether ``tqdm`` was imported or not. For instance
```
import tqdm
print("goo")

will throw the following error when tested with ``py.test --nbval  path/to/file.ipynb``

py.test --nbval .\test_nbval.ipynb

import tqdm
print("goo")

issue_title: Pass Django user when resource instantiated instead to every function call
issue_body:
I previously made a change to how `resource.get_fields()` is called by `import_action()` to take `**kwargs`, so that `get_fields()` would be able to change its behaviour depending on the Django user, like was being done by the call to `resource.import_data()`.

I'm thinking now that it is cleaner to pass `**kwargs` to the constructor for the resource object instead, on the line `resource = self.get_import_resource_class()()`. It's clearer for the resource to get the user in its `__init__` and store it, than to have the user passed as a parameter to every function call the resource has.

I'd be happy to make the change if you agree!

issue_title: Can't remove cA and then reconstruct
issue_body:
I have some EKG data i'm running wavelet decompisition on (thanks for this library!!). Here is an example of my code running level 6 db4 transformation on a 1 dimensional data set (list of voltage values, i.e. [1, 3, -1, 2...], the time intervals are +0.003s).

I used wavedecn because later I want to use waverecn later (where my problem begins)

```
wavelet = 'db4'
levels = 6
mode = 'constant'
coeffs = pywt.wavedecn(cA, wavelet, level=levels, mode=mode)
```

So I rebuilt using all the levels to get the original signal back. That was fine.
```
rebuilt = pywt.waverecn(coeffs, wavelet, mode=mode)
plotWave(rebuilt, "rebuilt1", "hopefully correct indices")
```

Then if I try to remove cA and cD6
```
coeffs.pop(6)
coeffs.pop(0)
rebuilt = pywt.waverecn(coeffs, wavelet, mode=mode)
plotWave(rebuilt, "rebuilt2", "hopefully correct indices")
```

I get all sorts of errors. If I pop cA `coeffs.pop(0)` or set it to an empty list `coeffs[0] = []` I get, "_All coefficients must have a matching number of dimensions_", if I set `coeffs[0] = None` it says "_The following detail coefficients were set to None: ['a']._"

I fixed my error by [forking your library](https://github.com/samyachour/pywt/commit/0182e30e1826d976103140ccb4df118391a17d32) and using my version, changing the code on [line 40 in multilevel.py](https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/master/pywt/_multilevel.py#L470)
from:
```
a, ds = coeffs[0], coeffs[1:]
```
to
```
# if we pop(0) and remove cA, all the elements will be of type dictionary
if type(coeffs[0]) is dict:
        a = None
        ds = coeffs
    else:
        a, ds = coeffs[0], coeffs[1:]
```
I'm not 100% what effect I had, my code works now and I can remove cA, c1, and c6 and everything runs fine. I think before [_fix_coeffs(coeffs)](https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/master/pywt/_multidim.py#L197) had a problem with setting cA to none (which I want to do if I want to ignore it in reconstruction), so I set it programmatically in the library. Is this correct? Why did my change work?

issue_body:
When output data is mixture of numbers and str (pandas object) MultipleFeaturizer converts all of them to object which is pretty bad. For example, the following test under "test_composition.py" would fail.

```
def test_multifeat_dtypes(self):
    featzers = [
        Stoichiometry(),
        AtomicOrbitals()
    ]
    multifeat = MultipleFeaturizer(featzers)
    df = multifeat.featurize_dataframe(self.df, col_id='composition')
    self.assertEqual(set(df.dtypes.astype(str)), {'float64', 'int64', 'object'})
```

if I override get_dataframe inside MultipleFeaturizer, the problem is fixed (also the progress bars work for each featurizer as opposed to only for MultipleFeaturizer). However, it seems like it fails some caching tests. @WardLT , this seems to be related to your code? Unfortunately, at the moment, I don't have a solution to both these issues:

```
def featurize_dataframe(self, df, col_id, **kwargs):
    multiindex = kwargs.get("multiindex", False)
    for i, featzer in enumerate(self.featurizers):
        if i>0 and multiindex:
            col_id = ('Input Data', col_id)
            multiindex = False
        df = featzer.featurize_dataframe(df, col_id, **kwargs)

```

issue_title: Source of spikes from multicomp cell is ~random segment if none named 'soma'
issue_body:
[This code](https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/cell.py#L543) in associateGid() for placing a NetConn which will be the source of spikes for that cell's gid:

```
 sec = self.secs['soma'] if 'soma' in self.secs else self.secs[self.secs.keys()[0]] # use soma if exists, otherwise 1st section
```

will try to select the 'first' section if there is none called 'soma'. However there is no guarantee that the key returned by self.secs.keys()[0] will be for the root section, it depends how the cell was created and how the list ordering is handled. 

It might be better to look through `self.secs` to find the section which has no parent (topol = {}) and use this:
```
 for s in self.secs:
     if len(self.secs[s]['topol'])==0: # i.e. no parent 
          sec = self.secs[s]
```

Thoughts @salvadord?

issue_title: examples/posix/flash.c problems with self-checks.
issue_body:
Technically there is nothing wrong with this, but depending upon the machine validation check-tests run on, the tests fail.  The current result, depending on the environment where the self-test operations are run they fail - randomly, but run perfectly when the next time.

The intent here is to fix/address the problem so that the validation tests work on more machines.

During the FLASH.C init() call, the simulated flash is erased - the current configuration is a simulated 256K byte flash chip (page size: 0x800(2K), npages=128 = 256K) 

Underlying problem:  The ${openthread}/tests/scripts/thread-cert directory - is run with numerous test scripts in parallel.  For example  Cert_5_2_06_RouterDowngrade.py - launches 26 instances of the CLI, Cert-5_2_03_LeaderReject2Hops.py launches 33 instances - the point here is there is a huge number of instances of the CLI application running in parallel.

The above to factors together cause the errors.

Solution/Option that is rejected:  Changing theses tests to run in *SEQUENCE* (not parallel) takes, means some tests take more then 3 hours to complete on the Travis-CI servers, well beyond a reasonable time limit. In fact, Travis dies with timeout errors.

Solution/Option that is rejected: Adjusting the total number of parallel jobs may solve the solution for one environment, but not all test environments, and extend test time on fast machines.

Solution/Option that is rejected: Just use a Bigger/Faster machines, this solution may work on sever class machine, but it is better if all developers can run the complete test suite on their assigned laptop, which may for example actually use a virtual machine. The developer tells the boss: I need a new laptop, the biggest, the baddest, the fastest thing on the planet.  The Boss says: What are you smoking?

Problem Detail 1 - The current implementation of page erase writes single bytes by calling pwrite()

Thus there are 50 instances of the CLI app - making 256K calls to the pwrite() function, each writing a single byte - depending upon the load on the machine running the test  (typically a VM of some sort) things timeout and fail for random reasons.

The solution is in this function *write* full pages in one operation, obviously (PAGE SIZE) 2048 times faster.

Problem Detail 2 - which is more of a comment of how *NOT* to fix this, and not cause other problems.  I learned this during my attempt to resolve the above issue.

To accomplish 1 (full page writes) - we need a PAGESIZE array of bytes, this could be: STATIC allocation,  MALLOC allocation, or on the runtime stack.

Static is not always a good solution.

The way to *NOT* solve this is to use MALLOC, meaning:  Allocate a page, initialize the page to 0xff, write the page, then free the page.

here's why:  Some (not all) of the tests make use of the address sanitizer  (aka: libasan, or just ASAN) the address sanitizer has a feature to detect  use-memory-after-free. To accomplish this, the function free() is changed to *NEVER* release the memory, instead free() "poisons" the freed memory in some form but does not release the memory. The net effect, the ASAN feature becomes a rather massively large memory leak.

Part 1: Thus any application that makes *MANY* malloc/free calls will cause a huge pseudo-memory leak. The CLI applications do exactly this as part of the flash page erase operation.

Part 2: Because the CERT tests execute well more then 50 tests in parallel, each with their own "pseudo-massive-memory-leak" means the test machine requires a HUGE amount of RAM (or swap space) this, coupled with virtual memory paging often results in either (a) timeout failures, (b) random strange crashes in Python due to out of memory errors, or (c) random failures in the ASAN enabled tests due to allocation failures.

On travis-ci, if you run the test second, or third time it sometimes passes, but another combination of tests fail, what is generally consistent is exactly an "out-of-memory" error/failure due to malloc/free + ASAN.

The simple solution is, in this case to place the dummy page on the runtime stack,  and not use malloc/free, and not use static memory allocation.

issue_title: Consider alternative readline imports for OS compatibility
issue_body:
Mac OS X ships with libedit instead of libreadline.  libedit doesn't work well with Python's readline module.  So a user either needs to use homebrew to install GNU readline or they need to use pip to install the gnureadline module which is a version of Python's readline module which is statically linked to the GNU readline library.

So to help with Mac OS X compatibility for users that have gnureadline installed instead of libreadline, it would be good to have an import setup like so:

```Python
try:
    import gnureadline
except ImportError:
    import readline
```

issue_title: Remove optional dependencies
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/acme', 'name': 'acme', 'color': '888888', 'default': False}, {'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/code%20health', 'name': 'code health', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 418985762, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/standalone', 'name': 'standalone', 'color': '7070a0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I think we should consider this for our next release as it will dramatically simplify our code. If we remove `already_listening` as #3623 suggests, our optional dependencies have little to no benefit.

The biggest change this would cause is the `simple_verify` method in ACME for the DNS challenge either needs to:
1. Raise `NotImplementedError`
2. Always return `True`.

If any third party plugins/clients that use ACME use this method, we should probably just return `True`. Otherwise, we should probably do the former and consider removing the `simple_verify` method from all challenge objects.

issue_title: Event formatting in REST API
issue_body:
## Problem definition

We provide API endpoints to query events. Right now the blockchain events look different from internal events. We should decide and implement a common format for both.


Example event query for a channel:

```json
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 1968
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Fri, 20 Jul 2018 10:08:08 GMT
mimetype: application/json

[
    {
        "address": "0x7692624c0C43285D51c0DdDfb2A6388f5002762E",
        "args": {
            "channel_identifier": "0x733ec6eecea8fb9fc2cb2b31ef3ae28cb751beedf956de43cca6943a1155c011",
            "closing_participant": "0xfB398E621c15E2BC5Ae6A508D8D89AF1f88c93e8"
        },
        "blockHash": "0x350ab9f001dd70fb8d38fa0861c3f90b16b221b1645d4bcc0a3447817d073d54",
        "blockNumber": 3674268,
        "block_number": 3674268,
        "event": "ChannelClosed",
        "logIndex": 2,
        "transactionHash": "0x9551bb75aa64b604410d843a1ef67bc57282eda394f17f66c9048f673d2f381c",
        "transactionIndex": 3
    }
]
```

## Proposed solution

We can split the endpoints into different endpoints for blockchain events and different ones for raiden internal events.

### Task 1

So that would need modifying the current event endpoints and adding two more.

In the end we should have:

- /blockchain_events/tokens/:token_address
- /blockchain_events/payment_networks/:token_address/channels/
- /blockchain_events/payment_networks/:token_address/channels/:partner_address
- /raiden_events/tokens/:token_address
- /raiden_events/networks/:token_address/channels/
- /raiden_events/networks/:token_address/channels/:partner_address

### Task 2

These endpoints for the internal events should be used for debugging. So we should remove the check  `EVENTS_EXTERNALLY_VISIBLE` from [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/751a77c4ebcab341ad48a5ec1c8fbb56f8847bc4/raiden/api/python.py#L662) and [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/751a77c4ebcab341ad48a5ec1c8fbb56f8847bc4/raiden/api/python.py#L709).

### Task 3 [Separate issue]

And when we do that we will need to also create a new API endpoint to query the history of the channel that will be using the `EVENTS_EXTERNALLY_VISIBLE` as defined in this [issue](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1949)

### Task 4

Remove the block number from internal events

issue_title: Smoketest fix makes Raiden non-startable w/o geth installed
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The fixes for #1237 (#1353 and #1374) cause Raiden to be non-startable if geth isn't installed on the user's machine.

The reason is that the check for the geth binary is perfomed on module level (i.e. import time) and the `smoketest` module is being imported by the `cli` module. 

IMO the easiest solution would be to only perform the check for geth when actually running the smoketest.

issue_title: Option shared of steps with substeps
issue_body:
Option shared returns step variables to the master process but it is entirely sure what happens to steps with substeps. 

Basically, option `shared` evaluates and returns variables after the completion of a step. However,

1. SoS only makes sure `step_output` exists and contains the collection of substep output (`_output`).
2. For other variables, 
  2.a: if `concurrent=True`, the substeps are executed in separate processes and currently does not return any variable.
  2.b: if `concurrent=False`, the substeps are executed sequentially so the variable is the "end result" after all substeps. That is to say, if each substeps returns a different value, only the last value is caught.

So in theory the `shared` variable will have to be substep-specific, that is to say, 

```
[1: shared='a']
input:  for_each={'b': range(2)}
a = b
```
should return `a=[0, 1]` for value `b=0` and `b=1` for different substeps, and
```
[1: shared='a']
a = 1
```
should in theory returns `a=[1]` (or `[{'a': 1}]`) to reflect the fact that there is one substep, but this is not how this option works now.

issue_title: Creating actors slows after exceeding CPUs despite terminating actors
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Ubuntu 17.10/Ubuntu 16.04
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: source
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**: 3.6.3

### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
When creating and killing actors, actor instantiation slows once the number of actors created exceeds the number of CPUs. The issue occurs despite killing actors using `__ray_terminate__`  such that there is only 1 active actor.

### Source code / logs
<!-- Include any logs or source code that would be helpful to diagnose the problem. If including tracebacks, please include the full traceback. Large logs and files should be attached. Try to provide a reproducible test case that is the bare minimum necessary to generate the problem. -->
#### Code
```python
import ray
import time

@ray.remote
class Foo:
    def bar(self):
        return 1

def run_actor():
    f = Foo.remote()
    ray.get(f.bar.remote())
    f.__ray_terminate__.remote()

for _ in range(10):
    start = time.time()
    run_actor()
    end = time.time()
    print("{0:4f} ms".format(1000 * (end - start)))
    time.sleep(1)
```
#### Output

7.824421 ms
7.327080 ms
856.424809 ms
790.140390 ms
838.207245 ms
842.015743 ms

791.989088 ms

I'm running this on a machine with 4 CPUs. If I launch Ray with additional CPUs e.g. `ray.init(num_cpus=8)`, the first 8 calls to `run_actor()` are fast, and all following calls are slow even though actors are out of scope and should be terminated.

issue_title: Feature request: Accept any M3U8 without needing to identify a plugin
issue_body:
----

### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [ ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Simply allow processing a plain M3U8 file the same as it's processed once a plugin finds it.

**Cons**

- Is not entirely 'on mission'
- May need a special cli flag, which would be non-intuitive
- May need some re-writing at a deeper level if the logic of everything goes through plugins

**Pros**

- Allows users to use streamlink no matter the source instead of first deciding whether it's a supported stream or an M3U8
- The flag could be as simple as `--bypass-plugins`, which opens the door to other possibilities in future

issue_title: Maybe move_on_after(0)/fail_after(0) should immediately cancel their scope
issue_body:
It's tempting to think that you can fake a "check once, but don't block" semantics by setting up a 0-timeout cancel scope:

```python
async def fake_accept_nowait(self):
    with fail_after(0):
        await self.accept()
```

This is not how Trio's cancellation semantics are supposed to work – there's no guarantee that `accept` won't raise `Cancelled` before it even attempts the operation.

@sorcio points out in chat that this actually works surprisingly well though. In fact, it probably succeeds deterministically right now (!), because `fail_after(0)` schedules its cancel scope to be cancelled "immediately", but since it uses a timeout to do this it doesn't actually happen until Trio processes timeouts, which it doesn't do until after this task yields. And if you look at `trio._socket._SocketType.accept`... it doesn't yield until after trying a non-blocking `accept`. So right now things work by accident.

This is dangerous, because this is a tempting thing to try, and the sort of thing where people might end up depending on it because it seems to work. I've been tempted and stopped b/c I thought it through and realized it wouldn't work – but we can't expect everyone to have my level of familiarity with the subtleties of Trio's semantics :-) – and @sorcio's tried it and found it worked...

So, if we want to keep the current semantics, maybe we should special-case `move_on_after(0)` and `fail_after(0)` to *immediately* cancel the scope they create, instead of going through the normal timeout machinery. Of course it would still be possible to get a similar effect with `move_on_after(1e-12)`, but then at least it's obvious that you're doing something potentially race-y.

Alternatively, could we change our semantics to make this work? The obvious problem would be IOCP on Windows. If you use IOCP to implement `accept`, then the underlying operation is actually "hey Windows, please start an `accept` running in the background", and then blocking and going through the event loop to check if it's done. In practice, if an `accept` is ready immediately, then it'll probably complete either synchronously (IOCP operations are allowed to do that) or at least before we notice the timeout and call `CancelIoEx`. But it makes me nervous? I dunno, maybe this would actually be fine.

The bigger problem is that the semantics of "don't deliver cancellations until a task actually *blocks* rather than just checkpoints" are fundamentally problematic: if you have some heavy computational work to do in the background, then the standard way is to go ahead and do it and just make sure to checkpoint frequently so other tasks can run. If we start delaying cancellations in general, then it becomes impossible to cancel a task like this.

So if we were going to do this, it would have to a specific thing the user had to request, I think – and then it would be the user's job to make sure that you only use it for operations that either succeed or block quickly, like `accept`. `with trio.move_on_when_blocks(): ...`. That's... not *totally* implausible. Certainly it'd be possible to implement, so long as we wrote down a clear definition of the difference between a checkpoint and blocking – which I'm a bit reluctant to do, because adding more distinctions adds complexity. Though we've already opened that door somewhat with `trio.testing.wait_all_tasks_blocked`… but that's in `trio.testing` plus it lets you specify a slop factor exactly to handle things like IOCP `accept` needing a few microseconds to be processed.

Probably the immediate thing to do is to add the special-case to make zero timeouts actually instantaneous, to keep our options open, and then continue to think about this.

Cross-ref: #242; in some sense this and that are alternatives, since they both potentially provide more general ways to handle `XX_nowait`.

issue_title: Running idealised scenarios for non-CO2 emissions
issue_body:
Hello,

I've just started using FAIR in Oxford and I've run into an issue that Richard and I haven't been abkle to figure out. 

I want to run some idealised emissions pulses using FAIR. I succeeded when emitting CO2 only. I'm using the multigas version. This is roughly what I did:

Import RCP emissions (because I was running that before and had it set up). Set the emissions all to zero except for one pulse of CO2. Then I run the following:

C,F,T = fair_scm(emissions=emissions,useMultigas=True,scale=scale,efficacy=scale)

where 'scale' is a list of all zeroes except for the first entry, which is 1, because I want to use CO2 only.

This seems to work, in that the F and T look as expected. 

However, when I try and do the same for methane (ie I set a nonzero pulse of methane, and change scale so the second entry is 1 and the rest zero) then I get negative forcing and cooling that does not reflect the emission pulse. 

I've inserted a set of plots from this methane pulse run. In the concentration and forcing plots, methane is in yellow. I have no idea why it goes down so much, as this is simply a result of one giant pulse that I add at the start. Perhaps it is due to some assumptions in the model that I don't know about. If you have any advice on how to fix this, or to do a pulse emission of non-CO2 gas in a better way, then please let me know. 

Thanks
Michelle 

![ch4idealised](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23382921/38088348-6a38c9be-3353-11e8-80cb-9a23e13cb274.png)

HWM for SUB socket does NOT DROP data
issue_body:
As I understand, hwm setting for SUB socket will drop new data if it reaches queue limit and drop new data.
However, in my test, the queue size of SUB socket does not drop the new data. It just saves all data into the subscriber's queue.

These are my test codes:
= server =========================
import zmq
import time

context = zmq.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
socket.bind("tcp://*:5556")

count = 0
while True:
    count += 1
    socket.send("%d" % count)
    print count
    time.sleep(0.5)

= client =========================
import zmq
import time

seconds = 2

context = zmq.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
socket.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, "")
socket.setsockopt(zmq.HWM, 2)
socket.connect("tcp://127.0.0.1:5556")

while True:
    count = socket.recv()
    print count
    time.sleep(seconds)

In this test, the subscriber must print jumping numbers because some data are dropped. But it doesn't.
